title: The Macro Minute: Is Kevin Warsh a dove in hawks clothing or a hawk in doves clothing?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42, it's your skipper here at Darius Dale to present our macro minute for Wednesday, June 17th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, is Kevin Warsh a dove in hawk's clothing, or a hawk in dove's clothing? The short answer is no idea. This is the biggest known unknown in all of global financial markets at the current juncture. Investors will receive their first clues for resolving this puzzle at this afternoon's FOMC event, or they won't. And if not, that will be by Chair Warsh's design. In terms of analytical nuance, incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's post-meeting press conference takes center stage this afternoon amid a Fed currently on hold and likely to revise its statement in a hawkish manner. Warsh seeks to prevent messaging favoring brevity over detailed guidance. Critics caution that curtailed messaging risks market volatility since forward guidance readies investors for policy moves. Warsh counters that central banks must preserve flexibility, dodging rigid commitments amid changing economic and geopolitical conditions. Policy makers can, quote, can become prisoners of their own words, Warsh said in April 2025 lecture at the IMF. The academic research is on Warsh's side. Swaps see no 25 basis point rate hike until March of next year. In addition to being critical of the Fed's over-communication, Warsh has been critical of the Fed's monetary largesse throughout recent decades. Our market-implied market Our market-implied neutral rate model currently views the Fed as one or two rate hikes behind the curve, meaning that it must hike by that amount to remove its now accommodative bias and get back to neutral. One of the key risks in all of global financial markets is the fact that Kevin Warsh thinks the Fed's balance sheet is, quote, trillions larger than it needs to be. The Fed currently owns 30% of the marketable Treasury bond market, which is 3,000 basis points more exposure than Kevin Warsh would prefer. Even after years of balance sheet run-off, the Fed's footprint in the economy and financial markets is still historically large. Any plan to durably shrink the Fed's balance sheet must feature substantial financial sector deregulation, such as swamping bank reserves with Treasuries for high-quality liquid asset purposes, and leveraging up by commercial banks. Lastly, will the Fed end reserve management purchases, and if so, will it reinstate them if funding stress returns to the repo market are key questions with Kevin Warsh in charge and may persist in dovish net financing policy. For what it's worth, the annualized rate of reserve management purchases peaked 4 months ago at $667 billion and has since declined to $434 billion. As always, we'll wrap up with a question from our community here in the 42 macro dashboard. This one's titled predictions for tomorrow's FOMC. It says, "What are your predictions for the outcome of tomorrow's FOMC meeting and presser?" Here's the AI guess based on options data. Despite the recent political commentary, institutional quote unquote smart money is buying the dovish pivot narrative. Over the last week, we tracked a massive build-up in S&P 500 put options, which which puts command commanding nearly 67% of daily S5 options volume ahead of the meeting. The data signals a high probability of a hawkish hold. The Fed will likely point directly to the hot jobs report and sticky inflation as reason to lock in a higher for longer regime. Expect an initial equity drop as algorithmic market makers unwind their short hedges and drag the indices towards uh key put walls. So, uh before I even go on, by the way, this is terribly incorrect. This is uh this stuff there. You cannot use AI, which is the amalgamation of everybody's thoughts that they've ever put on the internet right now, to guide your investment portfolio. You don't want everybody guiding your investment portfolio. There are millions of terrible investors saying things about financial markets on Reddit, on Twitter, on TikTok, on Instagram, on YouTube. Do not Do not trust AI to guide your portfolio. Certainly not at this early stage of the of the the the the adoption phase. Uh Get so So, the it's wrong in several categories. One, it says the institutional smart money isn't buying at the dovish pivot there. Also, they isn't buying the dovish isn't buying the dovish pivot there. So, they were correct on that. My apologies. But, the the concept that the market makers will unwind their short hedges and cause the market to go down is is obviously just factually incorrect. That would be causing the market to go up. So, what's more than likely is if Kevin Warsh hits on anything dovish, we're going to see a massive unwind of this put flow. You're going to get the the delta hedging and panic flows associated with that in terms of pushing the market up. But, again, I'm not saying that's the highest probable outcome. As I said earlier, we have no idea. We don't know if Kevin Warsh is a dove in hawk's clothing or a hawk in dove's clothing. He's basically, you know, he's play he's painted both sides of the fence very effectively over the past few years and and throughout his career in and around the Federal Reserve. So, you know, we're going to get our first breadcrumbs this afternoon. I don't know if it'll be definitive in the context of him, you know, shunning the tail policy guidance. But, you know, at least we'll finally hear him speak, you know, as the new Fed chair. And then, lastly, wrapping it up, it says alternatively, if we get a surprise dovish tone, the sheer density of this put overhang means the market is coiled for absolute face-ripping short squeeze. So, that is correct. So, that that that's the human >> [laughter] >> the member here adding their human overlay, which is obviously accurate accurate and correct. So, you know, key takeaway I'll say on all this is is you don't have to to to trade every wiggle in the market to retire on time and comfortably and achieve financial freedom. I've been saying very clearly that, hey, we're in paradigm C. That's obviously very bullish. We had a sprinkling of paradigm D in the form of reserve manager purchases. That's obviously incrementally bullish. This market, we've been saying since September of last year, we've been having been bullish since since April last year. We've been saying since September of last year that the risk of the market bubbling is exceedingly high in the context of paradigm C and paradigm D. And so, there's no change to that views. So, whether or not you are trading today's FOMC, uh the real key takeaway is if if Kevin Warsh is not does not represent a sea change in the US monetary policy reaction function in a materially hawkish manner, then we're talking about a a real true uh 1999-like uh bubble uh in in the equity markets. And so, uh investors need to brace for that outcome in so much as they need to brace for the outcome that that we could see something that's much more akin to the summer of 1998. Uh so, check out our I want to say our June 6th around the horn for more details on that. So, wrapping up there, Darius Dale here presenting our Macro Minute for Wednesday, June 17th, 2026. Best of luck out there today, and best of luck to you, Mr. Warsh. Obviously, we've had monetary K-shaped monetary policy, K-shaped fiscal policy, K-shaped regulatory policy for decades, and it's obviously led to a unnecessarily K-shaped economy. This doesn't have to be the outcome. So, uh this is our first opportunity that we've seen in a really long time for one of those uh levers to be swung back in a direction that supports Main Street uh instead of uh Wall Street uh in DC. So, we'll see. Uh everyone [clears throat] have a wonderful day. We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
